sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see the yellow blocks are at positions (25, 34), (25, 37), (28, 34), (28, 37), (34, 34), (34, 37), (37, 34), (37, 37). These form two 4x4 square outlines - one around rows 25-28 and one around rows 34-37, both at columns 34-37.
Looking at the image, I can see yellow circles around certain positions. These might indicate the "target" positions where blocks need to be aligned.
Let me think about this differently. In level 1, clicking the red block shifted all colors cyclically. The goal was to make the top and bottom rows match. In level 2, clicking the red block also shifts all colors, but there are 3 red blocks. Maybe each red block shifts a different subset of colors, or shifts in a different direction.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the changes. When I clicked the middle red block (row 26, col 14), only rows 26-27 changed. When I clicked the top red block (row 17, col 20), many rows changed including rows 17-18, 20-21, 23-24, 26-27, 29-30, 32-33, 35-36, 38-39, 41-42, 44-45.
So clicking the top red block shifts ALL rows, but clicking the middle red block only shifts the middle row. This suggests that each red block controls a different set of rows.
Wait, but the middle row (rows 26-27) also changed when I clicked the top red block. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the changes more carefully:
- When I clicked the middle red block (row 26, col 14), only rows 26-27 changed.
- When I clicked the top red block (row 17, col 20), rows 17-18, 20-21, 23-24, 26-27, 29-30, 32-33, 35-36, 38-39, 41-42, 44-45 all changed.
This is confusing. Let me think about this differently. Maybe the red blocks don't control rows, but instead clicking them triggers a global shift. The fact that the middle row changed when I clicked the middle red block might be coincidental.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the changes. When I clicked the middle red block at row 26, col 14:
- Row 26: wwcbbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggc -> bbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggcww
- Row 27: same change
This is a left shift by 2 positions (one 2x2 block). The first block "ww" moved to the end.
When I clicked the top red block at row 17, col 20:
- Row 17: SScbbcppcYYcppc -> bbcppcYYcppcbbc (left shift by 2)
- Row 20: pp...bb -> SS...SS (different change)
- Row 23: gg...SS -> pp...SS (different change)
- Row 26: bbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggcww -> bbcbbcggcSScppcggcwwcggcggcww (different shift)
- Row 29: ww...ww -> ww...gg (different change)
- Row 32: ww...gg -> ww...pp (different change)
- Row 35: wwcSScbbcwwcbbcSScppcbbcggcSS -> wwcSScwwcwwcbbcSScwwcbbcggcSS (different shift)
- Row 38: YY...ww -> bb...bb (different change)
- Row 41: gg...bb -> YY...gg (different change)
- Row 44: SScSScppcppcgg -> ggcSScSScppcpp (different shift)
So clicking the top red block shifts all rows, but the shifts are different for each row. This is a global operation.
Let me think about what the goal might be. Looking at the image, I see:
- 3 red blocks on the left
- 3 green blocks on the right
- Colored blocks between them
- Yellow circles around certain positions
Maybe the goal is to align the colored blocks so that they match the yellow circle positions, or to make the left and right sides match.
Actually, let me look at the image more carefully. The yellow circles seem to be around the positions where the colored blocks are. Maybe the yellow circles indicate the "target" positions, and I need to shift the blocks so that they align with the yellow circles.
But wait, the yellow circles are at fixed positions (25, 34), (25, 37), (28, 34), (28, 37), etc. These don't move. So maybe the goal is to have specific colors at those positions.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on the bottom red block and see what happens.
